poster_title: Is Chain-of-Thought Reasoning of LLMs a Mirage? A Data Distribution Lens
authors: Chengshuai Zhao, Zhen Tan, Pingchuan Ma, Dawei Li, Bohan Jiang, Yancheng Wang, Yingzhen Yang, Huan Liu
paper_info: Arizona State University, USA; arXiv:2508.01191v3 [cs.AI] 13 Aug 2025

content: CoT prompting enhances LLM performance on various tasks.
Initial studies suggest CoT reasoning might be superficial.
This work investigates the robustness of CoT reasoning under distribution shifts.
CoT reasoning is often seen as human-like but has inconsistencies.
LLMs rely on surface-level semantics and token associations.
Performance drops significantly under distribution shifts.
Propose a data distribution lens to study CoT reasoning.
Dissect CoT reasoning via task, length, and format dimensions.
Introduce DataAlchemy, a controlled experimental framework.
Evaluate CoT reasoning across task, length, and format dimensions.
Use DataAlchemy to train LLMs and test performance under distribution shifts.
CoT reasoning is effective for in-distribution data.
Performance degrades under moderate distribution shifts.
LLMs generate fluent but logically inconsistent reasoning steps.
CoT reasoning is a brittle mirage that vanishes under distribution shifts.
Effectiveness is bounded by distribution discrepancy.
Further research needed to develop more robust reasoning models.
CoT reasoning is not as robust as initially thought.
Models need to move beyond surface-level pattern recognition.
Future work should focus on authentic and generalizable reasoning.

Fig2.caption: Framework of DataAlchemy. It creates an isolated and controlled environment to train LLMs.
Fig2.description: DataAlchemy 框架图，展示了一个隔离和受控的环境用于训练
